title: “How Do I Carry All This Now?” Understanding Consumer Resistance to Sustainability Interventions
hypothesis_4omini: Consumer resistance to sustainability interventions emerges not primarily from unwillingness to change individual behavior, but from the targeting of individual behaviors rather than social practices, which leads to excessive responsibility, unsettled emotionality, and destabilized interconnected practices.

title: A New Livestream Retail Analytics Framework to Assess the Sales Impact of Emotional Displays
hypothesis_4omini: The emotional displays of salespeople, particularly happiness, exhibit a negative U-shaped effect on sales over time, with maximum sales resistance occurring in the middle of sales pitches, suggesting that salespeople should sell with a straight face.

title: Bayesian Synthetic Control Methods
hypothesis_4omini: The proposed Bayesian synthetic control methods (BSCMs) provide better predictive accuracy and parameter precision than extant synthetic control methods (SCMs), as demonstrated through simulations and a real-world application, with findings indicating a 5.7% increase in retail price and a 5.5% decrease in sales due to a tax on soda sales in Washington state, along with a pass-through rate of approximately 121%.

title: Augmented Reality in Retail and Its Impact on Sales
hypothesis_4omini: The rise of Augmented Reality (AR) technology positively impacts customer engagement and transforms brand experience, leading to increased sales in retail.

title: Despite Efficiencies, Mergers and Acquisitions Reduce Firm Value by Hurting Customer Satisfaction
hypothesis_4omini: Mergers and acquisitions (M&As) reduce firm value by causing a decline in customer satisfaction, which outweighs any gains in firm efficiency, and this negative relationship can be mitigated by marketing representation on a firm's board of directors that helps maintain executive attention on customers.

title: Branding in a Hyperconnected World: Refocusing Theories and Rethinking Boundaries
hypothesis_4omini: The study proposes a new branding paradigm that accounts for the roles and functions of brands, the co-creation of brand value, and the management of brands in a hyperconnected world.

title: Commentary: How AI Shapes Consumer Experiences and Expectations
hypothesis_4omini: The interaction between artificial intelligence (AI) and consumer experience is critical for marketers, and AI has the potential to enable profound product and service improvements that enhance consumer experience, despite criticisms that may misattribute issues to AI rather than broader digital technology or societal factors.

title: Commentary: Inefficiencies in Digital Advertising Markets: Evidence from the Field
hypothesis_4omini: The experimental techniques for the robust measurement of ad effectiveness proposed in Gordon et al. have not been widely adopted by U.K. advertisers due to internal frictions rather than lack of access to data or deliberate actions by digital advertising platforms.

title: Commentary: The Case for a Healthier Social Customer Journey
hypothesis_4omini: The commentary explores the critical role of shared values and the influence of distal social others, or "traveling companions," on consumers' decision-making processes throughout the social customer journey, particularly in the context of managing conflicts between personal brand preferences and broader social network values, and the impact of hate speech on consumer engagement with social media platforms.

title: Communicating Brands in Television Advertising
hypothesis_4omini: The study finds that salience cues (e.g., logo) and benefit and attribute messages moderate ad effectiveness, with explicit cues being more effective than implicit ones, and visual salience cues being the primary drivers of ad effectiveness.

title: Competitive Effects of Front-of-Package Nutrition Labeling Adoption on Nutritional Quality: Evidence from Facts Up Front–Style Labels
hypothesis_4omini: The adoption of front-of-package (FOP) nutrition labeling leads to an improvement in the nutritional quality of other products in the same category, with stronger effects observed for premium brands, brands with narrower product line breadth, and in categories involving unhealthy products and higher competition.

title: What Holds Attention? Linguistic Drivers of Engagement
hypothesis_4omini: Linguistic features associated with processing ease and emotion significantly influence attention, with emotional language's effects being mediated by arousal and uncertainty, where anxious, exciting, and hopeful language holds attention while sad language discourages it.

title: Corporate Sociopolitical Activism and Firm Value
hypothesis_4omini: Corporate sociopolitical activism (CSA) elicits an adverse reaction from investors, as they perceive it as a signal of a firm's resource allocation away from profit-oriented objectives toward a risky activity with uncertain outcomes, influenced by CSA's deviation from stakeholders' values and brand image, as well as the characteristics of CSA's resource implementation.

title: Reality Check: Infusing Ecological Value into Academic Marketing Research
hypothesis_4omini: The editorial proposes that research in marketing should be evaluated by a new standard called "ecological value," which reflects the relevance of research to marketing stakeholders and ecosystems, emphasizing the importance of staying connected to real-world marketing problems to enhance the discipline's influence and publication prospects.

title: Down a Rabbit Hole: How Prior Media Consumption Shapes Subsequent Media Consumption
hypothesis_4omini: The rabbit hole effect occurs because of increased accessibility of the shared category, leading consumers to choose additional similar media over dissimilar media after viewing multiple similar media consecutively.

title: Evolution of Consumption: A Psychological Ownership Framework
hypothesis_4omini: The evolution of consumption driven by technological innovations can threaten psychological ownership, cause it to transfer to other targets, and create new opportunities to preserve it, particularly in the context of the sharing economy, digitization of goods and services, and expansion of personal data.

title: Extremity Bias in Online Reviews: The Role of Attrition
hypothesis_4omini: The study proposes that differential attrition, where potential reviewers with moderate experiences are more likely to leave the pool of active reviewers than those with extreme experiences, contributes to the emergence of J-shaped distributions in online reviews, and that an unincentivized solicitation email reduces review extremity, supporting the existence of both differential attrition and differential utility.

title: A Meta-Analysis of the Effects of Brands’ Owned Social Media on Social Media Engagement and Sales
hypothesis_4omini: The meta-analysis reveals that owned social media significantly impacts social media engagement and sales, with owned social media being more effective for new products than mature ones, and that emotional content drives engagement while functional content stimulates sales; additionally, a large social media community is not essential for boosting sales, and a less uniform approach is more effective in high power distance countries.

title: Frugality Is Hard to Afford
hypothesis_4omini: Liquidity constraints impede low-income households' ability to use intertemporal savings strategies, and when low-income households have more liquidity, they partially catch up to higher-income households' ability to use these strategies.

title: Full Disclosure: How Smartphones Enhance Consumer Self-Disclosure
hypothesis_4omini: Consumers are more self-disclosing when generating content on their smartphone compared to a personal computer due to feelings of comfort and a focused attention on the disclosure task, which has important marketing implications.

title: Who Receives Credit or Blame? The Effects of Made-to-Order Production on Responses to Unethical and Ethical Company Production Practices
hypothesis_4omini: Consumers with control over production exhibit lower purchase intentions for products made with unethical processes compared to those with no role in production, while this effect reverses for ethically produced products, influenced by feelings of anticipated guilt or gratification. The study also proposes a threefold conceptualization of responsibility as managerial levers.

title: Buy Less, Buy Luxury: Understanding and Overcoming Product Durability Neglect for Sustainable Consumption
hypothesis_4omini: Purchasing luxury goods can enhance sustainable consumption due to their durability, but consumers often neglect product durability when making purchasing decisions, leading to a preference for multiple ordinary goods instead of fewer high-end products.

title: Harmful Effects of Mental Imagery and Customer Orientation During New Product Screening
hypothesis_4omini: Adopting a customer orientation during the new product screening process causes managers to engage in mental imagery that biases their evaluations toward unrealistic optimism, particularly affecting those who are more vigilant in their evaluations.

title: Hospital Portfolio Strategy and Patient Choice
hypothesis_4omini: The hospital's portfolio strategy, characterized by both depth (focus) and breadth (related focus), positively influences patient choice by signaling expertise in health care delivery, with potential synergistic effects and heterogeneity across departments, payer types, and hospital profit status.

title: How Does the Adoption of Ad Blockers Affect News Consumption?
hypothesis_4omini: The adoption of ad blockers positively affects online news consumption behavior by increasing the quantity and variety of articles consumed, with effects being stronger for less-experienced users.

title: How Life-Role Transitions Shape Consumer Responses to Brand Extensions
hypothesis_4omini: Consumers under life-role transition have more favorable attitudes toward distant brand extensions than those not under life-role transition, driven by self-concept ambiguity that prompts dialectical thinking, which improves perceived fit and brand extension evaluation; this effect diminishes for near brand extensions, sub-brand architecture, and when consumers perceive they have resources to cope with the transition.

title: Inefficiencies in Digital Advertising Markets
hypothesis_4omini: This article identifies four market inefficiencies in digital advertising—ad effect measurement, frictions between and within advertising channel members, ad blocking, and ad fraud—that are poorly understood and manifest uniquely in digital ad markets.

title: In Goal Pursuit, I Think Flexibility Is the Best Choice for Me but Not for You
hypothesis_4omini: Consumers prefer flexible plans for themselves but rigid plans for others, influenced by emotional versus logical decision-making processes, and this preference can be altered to favor rigid plans through nudging techniques.

title: Is Distance Really Dead in the Online World? The Moderating Role of Geographical Distance on the Effectiveness of Electronic Word of Mouth
hypothesis_4omini: The geographical distance between online users is positively associated with the effectiveness of electronic word-of-mouth (eWOM), as closer followers are more likely to make purchases after being exposed to users' WOM, with social identification serving as a potential mechanism for this relationship.

title: Learning from Data: An Empirics-First Approach to Relevant Knowledge Generation
hypothesis_4omini: The empirics-first approach in marketing research can generate valid insights from real-world phenomena without necessarily developing or testing theory, serving as a stepping-stone to theory and paving the way for its mainstream acceptance.

title: Marketing Excellence: Nature, Measurement, and Investor Valuations
hypothesis_4omini: Marketing excellence is a strategy focused on achieving organic growth through priorities related to the marketing ecosystem, end user, and marketing agility, and it has a quantifiable positive impact on firm value, leading to abnormal one-year returns of up to 8.58%.

title: Marketing-Mix Response Across Retail Formats: The Role of Shopping Trip Types
hypothesis_4omini: The study predicts that marketing-mix variables, including prices, nonprice promotions, and brand line length, differentially affect brand shares across various retail formats based on the type of shopping trip.

title: Commentary: Mind Your Text in Marketing Practice
hypothesis_4omini: The article emphasizes the importance of incorporating qualitative analysis and careful consideration of text origins and dictionaries in text analytics for marketing, suggesting that these factors can significantly influence the outcomes of marketing analyses.

title: Navigating the Last Mile: The Demand Effects of Click-and-Collect Order Fulfillment
hypothesis_4omini: The demand implications of click-and-collect (C&C) order fulfillment differ based on the type of fulfillment method used (in-store, near-store, stand-alone) and are influenced by shopper characteristics, with no single fulfillment type dominating across all conditions.

title: Open Negotiation: The Back-End Benefits of Salespeople’s Transparency in the Front End
hypothesis_4omini: The symmetry in the front end of negotiations provides an opportunity to build trust, leading to customers spending significantly more in the back end when the cost is disclosed at the beginning of the negotiation and can be verified.

title: Optimal Microtargeting of Advertising
hypothesis_4omini: The adaptation of optimal control theory for microtargeting individual customers improves firm profits over existing ad scheduling methods by between 150% and 183%.

title: Leveraging Brand Equity for Effective Visual Product Design
hypothesis_4omini: High-equity brands benefit more from brand typicality but less from segment typicality than low-equity brands in the context of visual product design.

title: Portraying Humans as Machines to Promote Health: Unintended Risks, Mechanisms, and Solutions
hypothesis_4omini: Portraying humans as machines promotes healthier food choices among consumers with high eating self-efficacy but backfires for those with low eating self-efficacy due to unmet expectations of adopting a cognitive approach to food. Enhancing the ease of adopting this approach can mitigate the negative effects.

title: Wearout or Weariness? Measuring Potential Negative Consequences of Online Ad Volume and Placement on Website Visits
hypothesis_4omini: The study finds that varying degrees of wearout and weariness exist among consumers in response to online ad volume and placement, with an appropriate "profiling and capping" strategy potentially improving ad deployment effectiveness by 15%.

title: Price-Matching Guarantees as a Direct Signal of Low Prices
hypothesis_4omini: Consumers perceive price-matching guarantees (PMGs) as a signal of low prices, leading to less price-sensitive demand for PMG stores, which paradoxically allows these stores to charge higher prices, contingent on sufficient firm heterogeneity.

title: A Practice Perspective on Market Evolution: How Craft and Commercial Coffee Firms Expand Practices and Develop Markets
hypothesis_4omini: The novel theory introduced in this article theorizes market evolution as resulting from practice expansion, which is influenced by ongoing institutional complexity and contributes to increased product diversity, broadened skills and knowledge, and an enriched market meaning system.

title: Regulating Product Recall Compliance in the Digital Age: Evidence from the “Safe Cars Save Lives” Campaign
hypothesis_4omini: The regulator-initiated digital marketing campaign (DMC) improves consumer recall compliance, increasing the number of vehicles fixed per recall campaign, with effectiveness varying based on media coverage, age of recalled products, and time needed for repairs.

title: Service Robots Rising: How Humanoid Robots Influence Service Experiences and Elicit Compensatory Consumer Responses
hypothesis_4omini: Consumers display compensatory responses when interacting with humanoid service robots (HSRs) rather than human employees, driven by greater consumer discomfort, which enhances compensatory consumption, with boundary conditions including social belongingness, food health perception, and robot anthropomorphism.

title: Societal Spillovers of TV Advertising: Social Distancing During a Public Health Crisis
hypothesis_4omini: TV advertising with COVID-19 narratives positively influences social distancing behavior, especially in the absence of government policy interventions, while government ads have a nonsignificant overall impact but show a significant negative effect in the presence of such interventions.

title: Targeted Promotions on an E-Book Platform: Crowding Out, Heterogeneity, and Opportunity Costs
hypothesis_4omini: Targeted promotions increase promoted product sales but can crowd out purchases of dissimilar products, leading to heterogeneous effects on total sales and significant opportunity costs.

title: The Bright Side of Having an Enemy
hypothesis_4omini: In a channel setting, more intense competition can be beneficial for the manufacturer, retailer, and consumers, and a manufacturer may prefer a strong competitor as it can lead to higher profits and incentives to improve product quality.

title: The Charm of Behavior-Based Pricing: When Consumers’ Taste Is Diverse and the Consideration Set Is Limited
hypothesis_4omini: Behavior-based pricing (BBP) can help firms earn more profits than without it in a horizontally differentiated market, particularly when consumer valuation is low, as lower prices can expand sales to consumers with limited consideration sets, while the opposite occurs when product valuation is high. The difference in prices charged for old and new consumers under BBP decreases with consumer taste diversity if consumer valuation is low, and the reverse is true if consumer valuation is high.

title: The Context (In)Dependence of Low-Fit Brand Extensions
hypothesis_4omini: The acceptance of low-fit brand extensions is influenced by consumers' context dependence, where context-dependent consumers evaluate extensions based on benefit-based information, while context-independent consumers rely on extension fit regardless of the type of information provided.

title: The Effect of Content on Zapping in TV Advertising
hypothesis_4omini: Ad content significantly influences zapping behavior, with ad creativity reducing zapping and strong information focus and branding increasing it, while irritation mediates this relationship.

title: The Impact of Advertising and R&D on Bankruptcy Survival: A Double-Edged Sword
hypothesis_4omini: Advertising and R&D can either increase or decrease bankruptcy survival, depending on the level of influence that the firm's suppliers have on the bankruptcy court's decision; specifically, advertising increases bankruptcy survival when at least 35%-38% of the bankrupt firm's debt is from suppliers, while R&D increases bankruptcy survival when 18%-21% of the debt is from suppliers.

title: The Influence of Social Norms on Consumer Behavior: A Meta-Analysis
hypothesis_4omini: Social norms shape consumer behavior, with their effectiveness varying based on target behavior characteristics, communication factors, consumer costs, environmental factors, and methodological characteristics, and while the effect on approved behavior is stable, the effect on disapproved behavior has grown over time and is stronger in survival and traditional cultures.

title: The Polarity of Online Reviews: Prevalence, Drivers and Implications
hypothesis_4omini: The study finds that polarity self-selection, where consumers with extreme evaluations are more likely to provide reviews, is a significant driver of the polarity of online review distributions, and that this polarity reduces the informativeness of online reviews.

title: The Signal Value of Crowdfunded Products
hypothesis_4omini: Crowdfunded products carry signal value for consumers, leading to a preference for these products due to perceived higher quality and the belief that supporting crowdfunding reduces inequality, with the effect reversing in high-risk domains and being stronger among consumers valuing social equality.

title: The Sleepy Consumer and Variety Seeking
hypothesis_4omini: Sleepiness positively influences consumer variety-seeking behavior, driven by a need for arousal to maintain wakefulness, and variety-seeking behavior can partially reduce sleepiness.

title: The Visual Asymmetry Effect: An Interplay of Logo Design and Brand Personality on Brand Equity
hypothesis_4omini: The degree of asymmetry in logo design interacts with brand personality to affect brand equity, with asymmetrical logos being perceived as more congruent with exciting brands, leading to increased consumer evaluations and financial valuations.

title: Tolerating and Managing Failure: An Organizational Perspective on Customer Reacquisition Management
hypothesis_4omini: Failure-tolerant cultures have an inverted U-shaped effect on reacquisition performance, while formal reacquisition policies have a positive linear relationship and enhance the beneficial effects of failure tolerance on reacquisition performance, ultimately leading to improved firm financial performance.

title: The Minimum Wage and Consumer Nutrition
hypothesis_4omini: Increases to the minimum wage improve the quantity and nutritional quality of food purchased by minimum wage earners, leading to increased calorie purchases and a greater openness to trying new grocery items, although the average household does not improve the nutritional content of calories purchased.

title: Words Meet Photos: When and Why Photos Increase Review Helpfulness
hypothesis_4omini: Reviews with photos are more helpful, and greater similarity between photos and text increases review helpfulness by facilitating ease of processing, while factors like language difficulty or poor image quality can reduce this effect.

title: Virtual Reality in New Product Development: Insights from Prelaunch Sales Forecasting for Durables
hypothesis_4omini: The use of virtual reality in new product development leads to highly accurate prelaunch sales forecasts and fosters behavioral consistency in consumer preferences and buying behavior, while enhancing perceptions of presence and vividness.

title: Visual Elicitation of Brand Perception
hypothesis_4omini: The proposed brand visual elicitation platform enables firms to quantitatively analyze consumer associations with brands through visual collages, revealing detailed associations that can inform brand management strategies, including brand personality, equity, and potential collaborations.

title: When Does Corporate Social Irresponsibility Become News? Evidence from More Than 1,000 Brand Transgressions Across Five Countries
hypothesis_4omini: The extent to which a corporate social irresponsibility (CSI) event damages a firm depends on the coverage of the event in high-reach news media, influenced by variables such as brand strength, media involvement, and advertising practices, with significant financial losses observed in the stock market contingent on media reporting.

title: When Words Sweat: Identifying Signals for Loan Default in the Text of Loan Applications
hypothesis_4omini: The textual information in loan applications significantly predicts loan default, with specific language patterns indicating borrower intentions and personality traits, beyond traditional financial and demographic variables.

title: User-Generated Physician Ratings and Their Effects on Patients’ Physician Choices: Evidence from Yelp
hypothesis_4omini: Online physician ratings positively influence patients' physician choices by signaling physician quality, with patients responding most to information about physicians' interpersonal and clinical skills, and the effects being moderated by rating credibility, accessibility, and strength of other signals.

title: The Brand Relationship Spectrum: The Key to the Architecture Challenge
hypothesis_4omini: A coherent brand architecture, facilitated by the brand relationship spectrum, can lead to impact, clarity, synergy, and leverage in brand management, addressing the challenges posed by market fragmentation and changing business environments.

title: “Statistical Significance” and Statistical Reporting: Moving Beyond Binary
hypothesis_4omini: The authors propose moving beyond null hypothesis significance testing (NHST) as the default approach to statistical analysis and reporting in marketing and the biomedical and social sciences, advocating for a transition that addresses the principal problems associated with NHST.

title: Commentary: Music's Digital Dance: Singing and Swinging from Product to Service
hypothesis_4omini: The commentary explores the proposition that preserving psychological ownership should be a priority for marketers and firm strategy, particularly in the context of the evolving consumption of music from physical products to digital services.

title: Do Nudges Reduce Disparities? Choice Architecture Compensates for Low Consumer Knowledge
hypothesis_4omini: Nudges are effective tools for reducing choice disparities, particularly benefiting consumers with lower socioeconomic status, domain knowledge, and numerical ability, while "bad nudges" exacerbate these disparities.

title: Mega or Micro? Influencer Selection Using Follower Elasticity
hypothesis_4omini: The follower elasticity of impressions (FEI) is always positive and nonlinearly related to follower size, which firms can use to better determine influencer partnerships.

title: Mindful Matching: Ordinal Versus Nominal Attributes
hypothesis_4omini: Consumers are more likely to match others on ordinal attributes than on nominal attributes, and this effect is driven by beliefs that failing to match on ordinal attributes will lead to social discomfort.

title: Popping the Positive Illusion of Financial Responsibility Can Increase Personal Savings: Applications in Emerging and Western Markets
hypothesis_4omini: Deflating the positive illusion of financial responsibility can increase personal savings by motivating individuals to restore their perceptions of financial responsibility, particularly when superfluous spending is under their control and they are motivated to perceive themselves as financially responsible.

title: Who We Are and How We Govern: The Effect of Identity Orientation on Governance Choice
hypothesis_4omini: Different firm-level identity orientations (individualistic, relational, or collectivistic) impact governance choice, with unique governance problems arising from match and mismatch scenarios between a firm's identity orientation and that of a value chain partner.

title: Choice Architecture for Healthier Insurance Decisions: Ordering and Partitioning Together Can Improve Consumer Choice
hypothesis_4omini: Targeted choice architecture interventions, specifically the combination of ordering options from best to worst and partitioning the total set of options, can significantly improve consumer health insurance decisions under certain conditions, particularly when the best options are presented at the top of the list.

title: Commentary: The Future of Marketing Is Agile
hypothesis_4omini: The COVID-19 pandemic has accelerated the need for marketing agility, requiring marketers to quickly adapt to changes in the marketplace and customer base, which has become essential for modern marketing practices.

title: Marketing Agility: The Concept, Antecedents, and a Research Agenda
hypothesis_4omini: The study conceptualizes marketing agility as the extent to which an entity rapidly iterates between making sense of the market and executing marketing decisions to adapt to the market, identifying its antecedents at various organizational levels while cautioning that it may not be suitable for all firms and marketing activities.

title: Real-Time Brand Reputation Tracking Using Social Media
hypothesis_4omini: The study develops a social media-based brand reputation tracker that demonstrates brand reputation can be monitored in real time and longitudinally, and that this monitoring is connected to firm financial performance.

title: When “More” Seems Like Less: Differential Price Framing Increases the Choice Share of Higher-Priced Options
hypothesis_4omini: Differential price framing increases the choice share of higher-priced options by creating a focus on the price difference, leading to lower perceived expensiveness and greater choice share for the premium option.

title: The Impact of Subscription Programs on Customer Purchases
hypothesis_4omini: Subscription programs lead to a significant increase in customer purchases, with the effect being economically significant, persistent, and heterogeneous across customers, where only one-third of the effect is due to economic benefits and two-thirds is attributed to noneconomic effects, including a sunk cost fallacy.
